data.title: IREN: The AI Party Is Coming To An End
data.description:
Following Nvidia’s (NVDA) meteoric rise in recent years, investors started to look for similar companies in the AI field, which have the potential to also generate significant returns. Considering the increased demand for computing power, it’s not a surprise that upcoming neocloud providers like IREN Limited (IREN) have quickly gained traction and have been able to benefit from the ongoing generative AI revolution. IREN’s stock has already greatly increased in value last year as the company itself was able to sign multi-billion dollar deals to provide the additional computing power for the biggest tech businesses in the world.

However, as is the case with all the other neocloud businesses, IREN’s business model is extremely capital-heavy. Unlike Nvidia, which is not heavily dependent on debt financing and has very limited CapEx, IREN and its peers are required to spend significant sums upfront to have the opportunity to generate any returns. While IREN is certainly an AI-related company, its risky business model makes it extremely hard to generate solid returns in the long run. Because of that, we believe that IREN is more of a trading stock and not a viable long-term investment.

Profits Are Not Guaranteed

The rise of generative AI has greatly increased the demand for computing power in the last couple of years. As a result of this, IREN, which was mostly focused on mining crypto in recent years, has now become one of the most popular neocloud providers on the market.

This was possible thanks to the $9.7 billion deal with Microsoft (MSFT), which IREN signed in early November. The deal itself is expected to span over the next five years, during which IREN is expected to provide Microsoft with access to Nvidia’s GB300 GPUs, which it plans to install in its data centers in the foreseeable future. As a result of this, IREN’s annualized AI cloud revenue by the end of 2026 is already expected to reach $3.4 billion. For comparison, in Q1, before the deal was signed, IREN’s revenues were only $240.3 million and were not expected to significantly increase anytime soon.

For Microsoft, this deal gives it major financial flexibility. After all, it gives the tech giant access to the computing power at a time when the demand for it is relatively high, while at the same time, Microsoft won’t be required to spend significant resources to build and maintain additional data centers. That’s not the case with IREN, which needs to build those data centers before it’s able to generate any returns whatsoever. This means that it’s almost certain that IREN’s capital expenditures are about to significantly increase, negatively affect its ability to generate positive free cash flow, and there’s no guarantee that it will be able to generate decent returns on its investments anytime soon.

From one of the company’s latest presentations from November, we know that the CapEx from the Microsoft deal is expected to be $5.8 billion. Out of that sum, $2.5 billion could be financed by the GPU-backed debt, while the $1.4 billion could be financed via the convertible notes or some other non-debt-related sources. During the latest earnings call, IREN’s management reiterated that various financing options are currently being considered. Then at the beginning of December, the company announced a $2 billion convertible notes offering, which suggests that the debt-related option was one that IREN decided to use.

While debt financing itself is not the issue, the problem in this particular case is that GPUs are quickly depreciating assets in the current environment. They are also the most expensive items in a data center. Last year, during one of its earnings calls, Nvidia’s management stated that $35 billion out of a $50 billion per-gigawatt data center budget is spent on their GPUs. At the same time, the latest data suggests that the life expectancy of GPUs is between five to six years. What’s worse is that they can become obsolete even earlier, considering that newer and more powerful accelerators are expected to be released almost annually. Considering that IREN plans to increase its GPU fleet from 23,000 today up to 140,000 by the end of this year, there’s a risk that over time it could be left holding less powerful accelerators with limited ability to generate solid returns after spending significant capital on them.

Last year, Oracle’s internal document showed that renting the computing power is financially challenging, and some of its operations were even losing money. This could be the case with IREN as well. While its revenues will certainly grow as the demand for computing power is still there, there’s no guarantee that it will be able to make decent profits by renting that computing power. Considering that the CapEx is more than likely to increase, it’s unlikely that IREN will become FCF positive anytime soon, especially since it was already generating negative free cash flow in recent years. This will likely result in greater reliance on debt financing since the company continues to burn cash.

While this might not have been an issue a year ago when the market was still putting a premium value on most of the AI-related names, now the situation seems to be different. In recent months, investors began to question the massive AI-related spending and started to believe that we might currently be in an AI bubble. That’s why it’s not a surprise that IREN’s shares have fallen since their highs in early November following the announcement of the deal with Microsoft, which has the potential to aggressively increase the company’s revenues.

The Real Value of IREN

Because of all the risks described above, there are reasons to believe that IREN’s upside is limited and the rebound of its shares to the previous highs is not guaranteed. To figure out at which levels it could trade and what its intrinsic value is, we decided to create a valuation model.

In the model, we used a 3% perpetual growth rate, which is a rate that’s close to the historical inflation and GDP growth rate. The cash and total debt data have been taken from the recent earnings report.

The discount rate in our model is 13.73%, which we figured after calculating IREN’s cost of debt and cost of equity rates. When calculating the cost of debt, we used mostly IREN’s data from the trailing twelve months. For the cost of equity, we used a risk-free rate of 4.15%, a beta of 2.79, and a market return rate of 7.84%.

In the model, we also assume a significant growth of sales and earnings, as there’s a potential for hyperscalers to continue to sign new multi-year deals with neocloud providers like IREN to get access to additional computing power. The growth rates in this model are slightly greater than the overall consensus but similar to the growth rates that we’ve used when valuing CoreWeave, which is another neocloud company that also signed a deal with Microsoft to provide computing power. The tax rate in the model stands at 21%, which is the current standard corporate tax rate in the United States.

We also assume a significant expansion of capital expenditures in the following years since the CapEx from the Microsoft deal alone is expected to be $5.8 billion over the following years. However, CapEx as a percentage of sales is expected to decrease in comparison to the last year due to the accelerated growth of sales. We also assume a growth of D&A in the years to come due to the fairly quick depreciation of GPUs and the gradual growth in change in net working capital.

Under all of those assumptions, our model shows that IREN’s enterprise value is $12.37 billion, while its equity value is $12.44 billion. That translates to the intrinsic value os $34.39 per share, which is around 25% below the current market price. Although we still expect IREN to have explosive growth in sales in the years to come, the massive expenditures that the company needs to make to provide the additional computing power will make it harder for it to generate significant FCF anytime soon.

Some Upside to Consider

We might be wrong, and IREN could nevertheless reach new all-time highs in the next couple of quarters. A lot will come down to whether the hyperscalers will continue to pour more funds into the expansion of their AI capabilities. This is one of the things that helped the stock market to constantly reach new highs in recent years, despite the fact that inflation was above the Fed’s 2% target and consumer sentiment was significantly below its historical highs. Considering that Goldman Sachs forecasts that hyperscalers might invest more than $500 billion in 2026, while Bank of America expects the economy to continue to grow this year, there’s a possibility that the AI-related stocks will appreciate in value.

Final Thoughts

Although IREN’s shares have gained traction and significantly appreciated at one point last year, we believe that it’s going to become much harder for the stock to rebound to its previous highs in the foreseeable future. Despite the potential growth of the overall stock market in 2026, the AI-related risks that have been described in this article are unlikely to disappear anytime soon. Therefore, it’s likely going to become much harder for stocks like IREN to aggressively appreciate going forward. That is why we believe that IREN is not a viable investment for the long term.

data.title: Oracle: 'Buy' The Dip As Backlog Diversification Continues
data.description:
Of all the large-cap tech stocks that dominated the stock markets in 2025, perhaps the most volatile and visible was Oracle Corporation (ORCL), the database giant. Oracle captured investors' attention with enormous RPO growth, and an ambitious plan to hit over $140 billion in revenue from Oracle Cloud Infrastructure by FY30 (roughly tripling from the size of its current revenue scale across all products).

At one point last year, Oracle's shares had nearly doubled to above $300; but since then, more realism has set in, bringing the stock down ~40% from recent peaks. In my view, this is a great time to "Buy" Oracle on the dip.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Oracle in October, when the stock was trading near $300 per share (note that I have been a bull on Oracle for years and have not wavered on that optimism, though I acknowledge my buy call in October was ill-timed). The rapid waning of enthusiasm for Oracle is driven by a complicated set of factors. First, investors are concerned about the security offered by its backlog. Much of its buildup in backlog and its hopes on future revenue are pinned on a single large customer: OpenAI. Meanwhile, Oracle is also funding its huge datacenter build out with a mountain of new debt.

While I agree that the risk of rising capex is real, it's also shared among all the AI hyperscalers (Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and the rest have all made similar commitments). It's also priced into the lower share price. Meanwhile, the company continues to diversify its backlog with other customers. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on Oracle.

Backlog Buildup Is Supported By Various Customers; OpenAI Monetization Efforts

First, let's address one of the principal concerns that investors have: the security of Oracle's backlog. The surge in the company's RPO was one of the main reasons that investors bid the stock up to $300 in the first place; however, more recently skepticism has built up around how secure OpenAI's promises are.

The first thing to note is that Oracle, in its most recent quarter, has continued to grow. The company added $68 billion of net-new RPO within the quarter, bringing total RPO to $523 billion (up ~5x y/y). Importantly, the new contracts in the quarter were from companies not related to OpenAI, including Meta (META) and Nvidia (NVDA), which are both highly profitable businesses that are less speculative than OpenAI. Oracle's overall backlog exposure to OpenAI is estimated at ~$300 billion.

Importantly, we note that much of this backlog buildup in the near-term fills immediately available capacity, which is leading to near-term revenue acceleration. Per CFO Douglas Kehring's remarks on the Q2 (November quarter) earnings call:

Let me start with the impact of the added RPO that occurred in Q2 on our future results. The vast majority of these bookings relate to opportunities where we have near-term capacity available, which means we can convert the added backlog to revenue sooner. The result is we now expect $4 billion of additional revenue in FY '27. Our full year FY '26 revenue expectation of $67 billion remains unchanged. However, given the added RPO this quarter that can be monetized quickly starting next year.

We can already start to see the impacts of some of this revenue in-fill in the most recent quarter. As shown in the chart below: in Oracle's most recent quarter, revenue grew 13% y/y on a constant currency basis to $16.06 billion. Growth accelerated two points on a constant-currency basis (versus 11% y/y growth in Q1), while revenue from cloud services grew at an even faster 33% constant-currency pace:

We note as well that OpenAI, Oracle's single largest (future) customer, is taking steps to shore up its business. In mid-January, OpenAI announced plans to start testing ads for free users on its U.S. platform. This solves a core issue with OpenAI itself: the compute resources it's providing, especially to free users, sit well above the revenue it's making. Though OpenAI still remains private, and we don't have specifics on the monetization potential from advertising, we think it's a positive that OpenAI is taking steps to improve monetization - which improves its long-term viability, highly relevant to Oracle as a large chunk of future revenue is dependent on OpenAI's survival.

Capex Surge Is A Real Risk, But Also Give Oracle Credit For Rising Operating Cash Flow

The other major topic on investors' minds is the huge jump in capex that Oracle is sustaining in order to build AI capacity (which is intertwined with fears that some of this capacity may have utilization risk, if OpenAI fails to live up to its commitments). On the recent Q2 earnings call, CFO Douglas Kehring noted that Oracle is now expecting $15 billion more in capex this year, implying $50 billion in total FY26 capex (a meaningful 43% jump from $35 billion stated previously):

We now expect fiscal 2026 CapEx will be about $15 billion higher than we forecasted after Q1.

Yes, the headline figure is massive. But Oracle is exhibiting a tried-and-true adage: to make money, one must spend money first. While Oracle's capex buildout is pushing FCF negative (and requiring the company to issue new debt) we also have to recognize the fact that double-digit revenue growth is also leading to operating cash flow expansion.

In the prior two quarters, Oracle's operating cash flow jumped 17% y/y to $10.2 billion. Yes, that is more than outweighed by $20.5 billion in capex over the last six months (and the capex run-rate will increase, based on its expectations of ~$50 billion this year).

There are several different considerations here. The first is that we note that this level of apex will not be sustained forever. Oracle is pulling ahead capex from several years to build out constrained capacity today, but once it has built out enough capacity, we don't expect Oracle to keep spending ~$50 billion per year ad infinitum. In other words, Oracle is trading off FCF today in exchange for a profitable AI datacenter revenue stream in the future.

Second, we note that Oracle continues to explore a mix of buy versus rent decisions that may reduce its cash flow strain. In response to being pressed on capex on the Q2 earnings call, new co-CEO Clay Magourk noted that it's in talks with several vendors to rent their capacity instead of buying it:

Similarly, we have different models that we're working on with different vendors where some vendors are actually very interested in the model where they rent their capacity rather than selling their capacity. And as you can imagine, that comes with different cash flow impacts that are favorable and reduce the overall borrowing needs and capital required for Oracle [...]

We've been reading a lot of analyst reports, and we've read quite a few that show an expectation of upwards of $100 billion for Oracle to go out and kind of complete these buildouts. And based on what we see right now, we expect we will need less, if not substantially less money raised than that amount to go and fund this buildout.

Again, I don't disagree that heightened capex spending and debt needs are a true risk for Oracle. At the same time, as long as Oracle can fill its capacity with revenue-generating customers, its investments will pay off in the long haul.

Key Takeaways

With Oracle's latest correction, we note that the stock now sits on the lower end of the valuation spectrum among other large-cap tech stocks:

In my view, recent fears on Oracle's capex spending, plus the viability of its open backlog with OpenAI, are overblown. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: 3M: A Light 2026 Guide And Mixed Q4 Numbers, Reiterate Hold
data.description:
3M Company (MMM) traded lower by 5% immediately after reporting Q4 2025 results. The bottom line beat was paired with a revenue miss. What’s more, the company’s outlook left something to be desired following a decline in its Transportation & Electronics and Consumer segments.

I was neutral on the now $85 billion market cap Industrials sector stalwart back in October. In light of today’s decline, shares are up 7% since my Q4 assessment, outperforming the S&P 500 by a few percentage points. Today, I reiterate a Hold rating. I’ll offer a refreshed valuation, taking the Q4 results into account, and update the technical situation.

MMM in-line With XLI, SPY YoY

In January, 3M reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $1.83 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $1.80, while revenue of $6.0 billion, up just 2% from the same period a year ago, was a small $10 million miss. The Minnesota-based large-cap offered FY 2026 forecasts, including adjusted sales growth near 4%, with adjusted EPS seen in the $8.50 to $8.70 range, right where the consensus was heading into the January report.

Shares fell 5% right after the numbers hit the tape, marking the worst reaction to earnings going back two years. The options market had priced in a 4.9% earnings-related stock price swing, so the absolute change was as expected, and it came after a stellar 7.7% advance in the wake of the October Q3 release. Implied volatility will likely retreat under 30% today, while short interest on MMM is low at 1.49%. The current forward dividend yield is 1.74%.

Looking closer at the quarter that was, it was a rather ho-hum October through December period. The modest revenue miss and somewhat material EPS miss came with a 21.1% adjusted operating margin, up 140 basis points from a year ago. Its cash flow was solid, with $1.6 billion in operating cash flow and $1.3 billion in adjusted free cash flow for the quarter.

Now, as for the guidance, the management team sees FY 2026 adjusted sales growth of 4.0%, with organic growth of 3.0%. Operating cash flow is seen between $5.6 and $5.8 billion, with further margin expansion of 70 to 80 basis points. The tepid outlook came after a bullish EPS guide last time, so I think investors were hoping for a more sanguine set of 2026 figures. No such luck. Still, the firm launched 70 new products in Q3, so there is momentum heading into 2026 with the new CEO at the helm.

Indeed, executives focused on growth and execution recently at CES in January. In short, the company is shifting from its historically premium consumer electronics focus toward more mainstream, higher-volume products. Data centers were cited as a growing segment, currently contributing about 3% of total revenue and adding roughly 0.5 percentage points to overall growth. We’ll see what added color we get on that as 2026 progresses.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to dip a bit in FY 2026 compared to the year just reported. Sales growth, however, is less strong, rising at a rate very close to inflation. So, operating leverage will be key to monitor in the quarters ahead.

Still, the sell-side has turned more upbeat in the past 90 days, as evidenced by a high 15 EPS upgrades compared to a lone downgrade. I would not be surprised to see a few downgrades after the conservative FY 2026 guide was reported earlier today. Profitability trends remain robust with the company.

3M: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have updates to my October assessment. If we assume $8.70 of forward 12-month non-GAAP EPS (near the current consensus) and apply the same 17x price-to-earnings multiple, then shares should trade near $148.

The stock trades just under $160 as of this writing, so there is simply not a strong valuation case right now. I am hesitant to give MMM much more than a 17x P/E (the multiple on the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index) given the sub-10% EPS growth ahead.

3M: Near Fair Value Based on P/E and P/S Multiples

Key risks for 3M include margin headwinds that could come about if we see too-aggressive capex. I don’t see this as overly likely, but with the intense AI/data center buildout ongoing elsewhere, that could entice the company to take on bold capex.

I will be curious to see how the company handles some of its ancillary segments—spinoffs could be accretive. Meanwhile, the auto area remains highly competitive and uncertain as household finances remain strained among the bottom half of the wealth in the country.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a projected Q1 2026 earnings date of Tuesday, April 21 BMO. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With the stock trading down after the Q4 report, MMM’s technical situation is generally healthy, but there are downside levels to keep an eye on. Notice in the chart below that shares are now testing near-term support just below $160. The long-term 200-day moving average is down at $154, and that has been a key buying point during previous pullbacks (going back to May 2025). From a risk/reward standpoint, buying there could make sense.

But take a look at the broader uptrend channel. The black lines are not precise support and resistance spots. Rather, they underscore that there has been a series of higher highs and higher lows since October 2023. I also like that the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has ranged in a bullish zone between 35 and 70 since Q2 last year. Near-term resistance is at the all-time high, in the low to mid $170s.

3M: Bullish Broad Uptrend, Rising 200dma, Solid RSI Trends

The Bottom Line

I have a Hold rating on MMM. The blue chip is just not the value it once was, while growth trends are not incredibly impressive. Still, the technicals favors the bulls, with shares testing near-term resistance after the Q4 earnings report.

data.title: KeyCorp: Pivoting To Offense After A Solid 2025
data.description:
Shares of KeyCorp (KEY) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 19%. Its 2024 decision to raise capital has paid off, enabling KEY to accelerate its balance sheet transformation and improve margins. At the same time, credit quality has remained resilient, and loan growth has momentum heading into 2026.

I last covered shares of KeyCorp in October when I reiterated them as a Buy. That call has played out with shares rallying 24% since then. In fact, the stock has blown past my $19 price target as concerns about credit quality have continued to ease, enabling multiple expansion. While Key just beat Q4 estimates, shares were down about 2% in early trading, likely due to some profit-taking after a strong run. With new financials and this outperformance, now is a good time to revisit KEY to determine if investors should take profits or if the rally can continue.

In the company’s fourth quarter, KeyCorp earned $0.41, which beat estimates by $0.02. We continue to see the benefits of its securities portfolio repositioning with interest income growing while its balance sheet remains broadly stable in size. Increased capital markets activity is also helping fee revenue. Credit quality and capital remain clear areas of strength. While 2025 was a transitional year, the company is set to go more on offense in 2026, which should benefit shareholders.

Balance Sheet Mix Continues to Improve, Boosting NIM

Looking first at its balance sheet, deposits were up $1 billion from last year and up slightly on a sequential basis. In Q4, Key let $1.3 billion of higher-cost brokered CDs roll off, given strength in commercial client balances. With slow loan growth as it repositions that portfolio, Key has not needed to aggressively grow deposits given its 72% loan-to-deposit ratio. As a result, Key has been fairly aggressive in passing on rate cuts, with a 16bp drop in deposit costs to 1.81%. Over the cutting cycle, Key has passed on a strong 51% of Fed cuts to customers while maintaining client balances. Importantly, its noninterest-bearing balances were also stable in 2025, and I believe we have hit bottom here.

On the other side of its balance sheet, loans grew 2% from last year. By the end of the quarter, they were up $600 million from Q3, thanks to strength in business lending. This loan growth was offset by the ongoing decline in low-yielding residential markets, which Key is strategically allowing to run off its balance sheet, redeploying funds to higher-yielding alternatives. As this portfolio continues to shrink, the run-off will be less impactful, facilitating stronger loan growth in 2026 in my view. Loan yields fell by 10 bps from Q3 given the cuts in the Fed funds rate.

Relative to peers, Key has one of the higher-yielding investment portfolios, as its capital injection from Scotiabank allowed the company to restructure its previously low-yielding portfolio. Key’s portfolio is fairly large at $48 billion, giving it a liquidity-rich balance sheet. The portfolio’s yield is up 29 bps from last year. The company has $8.7 billion of securities maturing this year at a 3.98% yield, which it should be able to redeploy at higher yields. Aside from securities, it has $9.1 billion of fixed-rate swaps (used to hedge floating rate loans) maturing at just 2.78%. As these low yields roll off, we should mechanistically see continued improvement in Key’s margins and revenue, even assuming 2 more Fed rate cuts this year.

During the quarter, Key’s net interest margin (“NIM”) increased 7 bps to 2.82%. This helped drive a 3% sequential increase in net interest income. This was also up 15% from last year, reflecting the benefits in its securities portfolio from the repositioning. Given ongoing maturities and mix shift, we should see NIM continue to climb by about 5 bps per quarter in 2026.

Noninterest income was also up a solid 8% to $782 million led by strong growth in investment banking and trust services, which now has a record $70 billion of AUM. Given increased M&A activity (which I expect to persist this year) and strong equity market levels, these fees should continue to be strong in 2026. Key has also been able to grow deposit charges by 20% to $78 million, given fees on commercial balances, which has further helped to improve the profitability of its deposit franchise. There is risk that congress could reduce credit card interchange fees, but at only 11% of fee revenue, Key’s exposure is limited. Still, it could be a $20-40 million annual risk that bears monitoring.

Credit Quality and Capital Are Strong

Turning to credit quality, nonperforming assets fell by 6% to $627 million. They are now down about $145 million from last year. Net charge-offs were 39 bps, which is also the strongest performance in over a year. Key’s focus on lending to corporate clients who have a broader relationship with the bank (via deposits, treasury services, etc.) has helped to develop sticky relationships with borrowers whose business it understands well, reducing losses.

Even as NPAs have declined substantially, Key has added $41 million to allowances for credit losses over the past year, and they now total $1.74 billion. This provides excellent 277% coverage of NPAs, more than the 250% threshold I view as strong. Key has taken a cautious stance towards its reserves, which now gives the company significant buffer. In the event of an economic slowdown, Key is unlikely to need to add much more in reserves, and should the economy avoid recession (as I expect), we can see Key gradually reduce reserve coverage this year.

Key’s tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio ended the quarter at 11.7%. As noted above, the Scotiabank stake purchase enabled KEY to realize much of its losses on its securities portfolio while maintaining an adequate capital buffer. Including all remaining unrealized losses of about $2 billion, Key’s capital is 10.3%, above its 9.5-10% target. In fact given its capital position, Key was able to repurchase $200 million of stock during the quarter. Buybacks should continue to accelerate with management targeting $1.2 billion of buybacks this year. Additionally, shares offer a 3.9% yield. Given Key’s excellent capital position, I view its dividend as highly secure.

Highly Credible Earnings Growth Makes Key Attractive

Alongside results, Key provided initial guidance for 2026. Key expects to grow average loans by 1-2%. I expect the company to deliver at the high-end of this, given a shrinking headwind from residential mortgages and strong commercial demand as economic uncertainty has come down from last year. Notably, revenue is expected to outpace balance sheet growth substantially with NIM passing 3% by year end, on track to hit the 3.25% target by the end of 2027. As noted above, Key has highly certain NIM tailwinds given the maturity of low-yielding swaps and securities. Plus, the rotation from mortgages to C&I lending will be accretive to its loan yield.

This gives Key one of the clearest NIM expansion stories in the sector, and I view this guidance as highly credible, barring more than 3 Fed rate cuts. Management also expects charge-offs to be similar to the 41 bps in 2025, speaking to a broadly stable credit environment.

Back in October, I saw 2026 results tracking towards the upper end of my $1.80-$1.90 target. With share buybacks likely to help reduce the share count, I now see Key earning $1.80-$1.95, assuming 2-3 Fed rate cuts and stable provisions for credit losses. Shares are now ~11x earnings, which is comfortably within the 9-13x range I view as appropriate for regional banks in this environment.

With its strong capital position, accelerating buybacks, and clear path for NIM accretion, I view shares as still attractive within this range and expect them to migrate towards 12x. This points to a $22.75-$23 fair value, providing 10+% upside including its dividend. As such, I remain a Buy on Key and would use any softness in shares to add.

data.title: Willis Lease Finance, Bnccorp, And California First Leasing Updates
data.description:
I'm a long-term investor who rarely has short-term stock trade ideas. This routinely causes confusion. Since exiting bankruptcy, Core Scientific, Inc. (CORZ) has been a core position that I first disclosed in Bitcoin And The Best Miner: Core Scientific.

However, when mentioning this on a recent podcast, one of the reactions was that the idea hadn't worked that day—not by that day but literally was down (apparently, I literally didn't check) since 9:30 AM that morning. He seemed to regard this as a very clever point. I rarely have much to say to such people besides a half-hearted effort at guessing if they're sarcastic or innumerate. Today, I do! In three cases, long-term investments have short-term trades that people who like such things can exploit for profit.

Willis Lease

I've written up Willis Lease Finance Corporation (WLFC) twenty times on StW since I first disclosed the position and then subsequently disclosed it publicly.

It was an extraordinary opportunity at the time. After a bad 2025, it is again extraordinary for 2026. Last month, I mentioned that,

It has raced in recent years, but gave back over a third of it in 2025. There could be tax loss selling this month then a rebound in the New Year. Now could be an opportune time to add or newly buy.

So whether or not I'm right about my long-term thesis on WLFC, January 2026 could offer a good trade on the relief from tax loss selling. But there's more. The company recently announced a series of partnerships that will allow it to scale quickly. They are transforming the business model into an engine leasing asset management business. Medium-term, it is likely to regain 2024 highs. Long-term, it could converge on FTAI Aviation's (FTAI) market cap, which is currently >20x as big. But of potential interest to short-term-oriented traders: near-term they are likely to sell their engine inventory to these partnerships in an asset sale that would unlock about two-thirds of today's market cap. The headline could cross any day and would rerate the stock, probably to the upper end of a $200-250 per share range.

BNCCORP

This comes in third behind the aforementioned CORZ and WLFC in the StW ideas.

I've long ago gotten my entire cost basis back in distributions. But house money is still money, so I still follow it carefully. Because of large special dividends, it has done fine since I first publicly disclosed it, but it has taken far longer than I wanted or expected.

At the time, it was trading around book value, which it was growing. My plan was to encourage a sale and add it to our list of banks that sold after our encouragement, including SSE, OBA Financial (nee OBAF), CMS Bancorp (nee CMSB), Ocean Shore (nee OSHC), and others. Book value has crept up from ~$17 at the time to ~$30 today. Both the management and board have been upgraded. It is worth at least $40 per share to even the stingiest strategic bidder and well over $45 per share in the best deal, which is probably to a mutual such as Gate City Bank either in cash or in stock in their potential demutualization. This is the year. Regulators are as benign as they have ever been or might ever be again. There could be a political backlash in 2026 and again in 2028 that might slam the door shut on such deals. Now's the time.

I started the year with the concern that the board needed shareholder encouragement to sell now. But today my view is that no further shareholder actions are necessary. They got the message; nothing more to be said or done. While we have not seen a dividend this month, I don't expect another special distribution, nor do I object to not getting one. The board is aligned with shareholder interests and will get us the best deal, hopefully in time to close this year before the political climate could shift against us.

California First

A few years ago, I wrote that,

California First Leasing Corporation (CFNB) is a small family controlled company with a book 75% invested in liquid blue chip stocks. They have done a self-tender offers per year for the past few years and are likely to continue at that rate in an effort to close the discount to NAV. Shares trade over the counter and cost $16.75, a 27% discount to their $23 net asset value. This is worth a 5% position.

This is a weird one. The (married) CEO and CFO essentially treat the company as their family office and the small publicly traded float as an irritation. NAV has crept up from ~$23 to $34 of extremely liquid large-cap equities that could be sold without any friction within minutes. Cynically, the company is trying to get around transparency requirements of the 1940 Act by cashing out small shareholders in order to get under 100 shareholders. This tactic is easily defeated by a simple step: if you get cashed out, take that cash and buy the shares back! They would like to get rid of us without paying us a premium. But if their maneuver is defeated by shareholders buying and then rebuying shares, they will be left with fewer tricks to avoid paying us a premium that meaningfully closes that $7 gap between the market price and the share asset value. The definitive proxy states that

After consolidating shares held by affiliated parties, the number of discrete shareholders is estimated to be approximately 134. If the Board elects to implement the Reverse Stock Split, we expect that cashing out positions with less than 50 shares and fractional shares would reduce the estimated number of shareholders to just under 100.

This is incorrect. The number of discrete shareholders is currently significantly above 134. Further, if they implement the reverse stock split, cashing out positions with less than 50 shares and fractional shares would not reduce the number of shareholders to under 100. The proxy continues,

There is no assurance or guarantee that the number of shareholders after the Reverse Stock Split would fall and remain under 100.

To strengthen that point, it would not fall and remain under 100 because shareholders are not so easily tricked. They/we can just buy the shares back.

Then what? Then they are quickly running afoul of tax rules around Personal Holding Company (PHC) tax rules. This is a C Corporation where over 50% of shares are owned by five or fewer individuals and over 60% of income is from passive sources such as dividends, interest, rent, or royalties. CFNB unambiguously qualifies. This will trigger a special 20% PHC tax on undistributed income. To avoid that unforced error, they will have to make a substantial distribution by the end of next quarter. Such distributions are especially advantageous when shares are at NAV discounts because they help capture that discount.

The current situation is untenable for everyone involved. There is no reason for this to remain public beyond this year. There is no equitable way to compensate shareholders that avoids endless expensive litigation with a proctological level of discovery besides simply paying ~NAV in a short-form merger. Instead of his and hers lawsuits for the CEO and CFO, they could just pay the minority and formally turn this company into the family office that it already is.

Caveats

I think that there's a fast, lucrative trade in all three, but that's not why I own them. I've owned them for years and just stumbled on near-term events that could cause these stocks to roar in 2026.

Things take longer than I want or expect, both generally and in these three cases. Steps to unlock shareholder value have been so clear that I thought they'd have happened by now in theory. In practice, I was early, and the timing is right now.

Conclusion

Do with this what you will—WLFC has a clean shot at $250. BNCC has a clean shot at $45. CFNB has a clean shot at $34. Personally, I think that these are highly edgy, actionable ideas.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nvidia: Buy The 15% Dip, Improved Valuation Amid Macro Worries
data.description:
A global selloff intensified to begin the week, just as economic heavyweights and power players gather in Davos, Switzerland. Tariffs and Greenland are in the spotlight. Shares of Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) sank more than 2% in the premarket on Tuesday, January 20. That deepened the world’s most valuable company’s drawdown from its all-time high to about 15%. Specifically, NVDA traded lower on Tuesday, January 20, as a report surfaced that suppliers halted production of H200 chip components.

I had a buy rating on Nvidia back in October. Shares are down 10% since then, lagging the S&P 500 by 11 percentage points. Bigger picture, NVDA is still up more than 39%, dividends included, over the past year, outperforming the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Information Technology Select Sector SPDR (XLK).

I reiterate a Buy rating on NVDA. I’ll offer a refreshed valuation, with fiscal Q3 numbers in the books, and an updated view of the technicals ahead of its February Q4 earnings report.

S&P 500 Drawdown from 52wk High Heat Map: NVDA -15%

NVDA Outperforming YoY

NVDA Steadies In Terms of Global Equity Weighting

Back in November, Nvidia reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $1.30 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $1.26, while revenue of $57 billion, up a whopping 63% from the same period a year ago, was a significant $1.9 billion beat. Jensen Huang’s company tallied $51.2 billion in Data Center revenue, a 25% sequential increase, and up 66% from its Q3 2025 (fiscal period). Nvidia saw its fiscal Q4 revenue above estimates after the Q3 numbers hit.

Shares fell by 3.2% in the session that followed, however, marking a second straight negative reaction to earnings. Looking ahead to the Q4 report due out on February 25, the options market prices in a somewhat low 5.8% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the release. Implied volatility ended last week at 35.5%, though it has increased amid the global selloff today. The next dividend is slated for March 12, while short interest on NVDA is low at 1.06%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, the Q3 numbers were stellar. Core demand for Nvidia’s suite of chips and platforms was broadly better than estimates, with visibility on future earnings and free cash flow being made clearer. In fact, its Q3 free cash flow surged 60% sequentially to $22 billion—the second highest on record for the $4.5 trillion market cap company.

Why the trade-down post-earnings? Perhaps it was due to concerns related to its rising receivables and inventory levels, but I think the street focused too much on that piece. Given recent reports (such as Taiwan Semiconductor's (TSM) latest quarterly update), demand is likely outstripping supply in the aggregate.

Of course, rising competition from in-house and custom chip players has taken the froth out of the glamour stock. Still, I expect stellar figures regarding Blackwell GB200/GB300 to be confirmed next month, and we already heard some bullish comments from CEO Jensen Huang at CES recently.

“Insane Demand for AI Computing”

On the call, executives were upbeat, raising Q4 top-line guidance to $65 billion. More broadly, the management team sees $500 billion of revenue of AI infrastructure through this year. Still, it will be key to watch margins and cost trends through the next few quarters. What’s more, renewed tariff tensions could pressure the stock (as we saw from February through early April last year). Nvidia may also have to take part in power-market bidding to foot some of the AI electricity costs.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to surge about 60% YoY in FY 2027, with some growth deceleration in the out year. Revenue gains are likewise stout, continuing a trend that began three years ago.

As such, there have been a high 40 sell-side EPS upgrades in the past 90 days, compared to just a lone downgrade—all while the stock price has dropped significantly. That’s a prime setup for an improved valuation. Indeed, profitability trends are best in class—Nvidia has generated $3.17 in free cash flow per share in the past 90 days, even with heavy capex.

Nvidia: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

Big TTM Free Cash Flow Growth

On valuation, I have modest updates to my figures from last October. If we assume $7.60 of non-GAAP EPS over the next 12 months (near the current consensus) and apply a 30x price-to-earnings ratio I used last time, then shares should trade near $228, making NVDA undervalued.

I took down the earnings multiple, given the higher likelihood that the firm is closer to peak margins. Still, the PEG ratio is barely above 1—a solid GARP idea.

NVDA: Not Expensive on Forward Earnings

Key risks for Nvidia include rising competition from other chip designers, along with semiconductors and AI programs developed in-house by major tech companies and hyperscalers. Moreover, the global supply chain could be pressured amid renewed tariff concerns and higher costs of production.

China is, of course, a natural worry, as trade tensions between the U.S. and the world’s second-largest economy have not improved much. The credit markets must also be monitored, as the AI buildout is increasingly debt-funded. Global yields have increased lately.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2026 earnings date of Wednesday, February 25 AMC. Before that, the company presents at the SCA/HPCAsia 2026 conference beginning on Monday, January 26. The firm’s fiscal year 2026 ends this coming Sunday.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

NVDA: Bullish Q1 Historical Trends

The Technical Take

With shares now a compelling fundamental value after earnings strength and a stock price decline, NVDA’s technical situation is not ideal. Notice in the chart below that a bearish head and shoulders topping pattern could be playing out. But rather than immediately shorting shares based on a potential chart setup, investors must wait for a break of the neckline, currently at $170. That’s also where the long-term 200-day moving average comes into play. So, we have a clear support spot to monitor.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It printed a bearish divergence to price at the October high, back when AI and tech stocks peaked compared to the global equity market. Longer-term support is down at the $153 mark—the range highs from 2024. I’d like to see NVDA rise above $195 to help negate the head and shoulders pattern.

NVDA: Head and Shoulders Pattern is Ominous, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on NVDA. I see the world’s most valuable company by equity market cap as more attractive on valuation, while the technical situation is precarious ahead of its Q4 earnings report next month.

data.title: What 2025 Taught Us About U.S. Banks: Insights For A Surprising 2026
data.description:
Introduction

We are only three weeks into 2026, and the major U.S. banks were among the first companies to release their results for the fourth quarter and full year 2025. The share price reactions of the Big 4, namely JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Bank of America Corp. (BAC), Citigroup Inc. (C), and Wells Fargo & Company (WFC), to the earnings raises the question of whether it was the results for 2025 or rather the outlook for the new year that disappointed investors.

I've written about the U.S. banking industry as a whole on several occasions here on Seeking Alpha, but have focused on smaller (yet still significant) banks such as U.S. Bancorp (USB) and Truist Financial Corp. (TFC). In my last more general article on the banking sector, published in January 2025, I discussed macro indicators and regulatory aspects that I consider relevant for investors in U.S. banks. In this article, I will focus on the 2025 results of JPM, BAC, C, and WFC and share my expectations for the industry going forward. The discussion will be rounded off by the inclusion of U.S. Bancorp, currently the fifth-largest bank in the U.S. in terms of consolidated assets. As the bank had not yet published its results for 2025 at the time of writing (results are scheduled to be released today at 8 a.m. CT), I will use trailing twelve months or year-to-date figures from the last twelve months for comparative purposes.

2025: Another Strong Year For The U.S. Banking Sector – Do The Post Earnings Reactions Signal Investor Caution?

As illustrated in Figure 1, BAC and WFC delivered adjusted earnings per share (EPS) largely in line with estimates, while C's earnings for 2025 came in more than 5% below consensus. Interestingly, also JPM missed on earnings – something we rarely see, but in fairness the miss wasn't really significant at -1.4% vs. consensus.

Looking a bit closer at the rare earnings miss by the #1 bank in the U.S., I was rather impressed by the continued solid performance, especially with regard to its asset and wealth management business, which had almost $4.8 trillion in assets under management at the end of Q4 2025, thanks to ongoing solid net inflows (and of course due to strong market performance). Related net income grew 9% year-over-year in Q4, and this business line is becoming an increasingly meaningful contributor to overall net income (13.9% in Q4 2025). Net revenues grew by 7%, pointing to good operating leverage in this domain.

JPM firmwide loans were up 9% year-over-year and deposits grew by a very solid 6% - I think this is extremely impressive in view of the bank's already huge size. In Consumer & Community Banking, JPM reported deposit and loan growth of 1% year-over-year, but significantly increased provisions for credit losses (both sequentially and year-over-year) resulted in a 19% year-over-year decline in net income. In Commercial & Investment Banking, credit loss provisions were also up from a year-over-year perspective but remained very modest at only $405 million, suggesting still strong economic fundamentals among commercial customers while the former observation once again points to a weakening consumer.

Firmwide net charge-offs grew modestly compared to 2024 and the continued strong reserves suggest a cautious stance going forward.

JPM also remains the strongest of the Big 4 (or 5, if we include non-globally systemic important bank U.S. Bancorp) in terms of the regulatory CET1 (Common Equity Tier 1) ratio, as illustrated in Figure 2. For the sake of completeness and to better understand capital requirements, I also included the risk-based category in the figure, as well as to which G-SIB bucket each bank belongs.

In my view, all of the banks remain well capitalized and should be able to handle a significant downturn without facing major issues. All banks are capitalized well within their respective regulatory requirement. As an aside, the results from the 2025 Federal Reserve Stress Test can be found here.

From the perspective of loss-absorbing capacity and capitalization, it is of course also crucial to review asset quality. In this context, I like to look at net charge-offs, although I do concede that a direct comparison of the five largest banks in the U.S. appears more or less questionable due to the different exposure to consumer credit facing business channels. This is especially evident for Citigroup with its comparatively large credit card and retail portfolio (gray bars in Figure 3). That said, the year-over-year decline in net charge-offs was definitely good to see.

The increase in net charge-offs among most banks since 2021 is largely due to the normalization from the artificially low number of defaults observed during the pandemic (owed to, e.g., ultra-low-interest rates and loan moratoria) but of course the sharply rising interest rates and high inflation have taken their toll as well. That said, charge-offs remain at very well manageable levels and thanks to the banks' strong capitalization, I sense no systemic risk on the horizon. The continued focus on lean operations, which was adopted as a result of post-GFC low-interest rate environment, and the significant economies of scale are likely to continue to benefit these large institutions, as they enable them to build up significant reserves while continuing to distribute cash to their shareholders.

Speaking of which, in terms of efficiency gains, the ongoing efforts of both BAC and C are worth highlighting, in my view. Of course, BAC’s somewhat weak expense guidance for 2026 likely surprised investors a bit, but the bank nonetheless expects meaningful positive operating leverage for 2026, to the tune of 200 bps. There's still a long way until the previously announced efficiency ratio target in the high 50% range is reached, but BAC is making good progress. In 2025, its efficiency ratio improved by more than 1.4 percentage points, to 61.3%.

Competitor Citi saw its efficiency ratio improve by 1.7 percentage points, but the ratio is still at almost 65%. Wells Fargo continues to disappoint from the perspective of operating efficiency, but management's 2026 operating leverage guidance makes me optimistic that things will improve going forward.

The comparison in Figure 4 shows why I personally like U.S. Bancorp a lot. While the bank certainly has its own issues and quite a bit of room for further improvement, it's the still comparatively high agility that sets this smaller non-G-SIB apart from the others from an efficiency perspective. This is also evident from Figure 5, which compares the historical returns on tangible common equity (ROTCE).

In addition to ROTCE, the net interest margin (Figure 6) also confirms that JPM is the best positioned of the five largest banks in the U.S. As the largest domestic bank, it can simply afford to pay comparatively lower interest rates on its deposits and is generally able to borrow more cheaply than perceived riskier competitors.

In my view, short-term interest rates will continue to decline while longer-term rates could continue to remain sticky. While this naturally benefits banks' earnings from a maturity transformation perspective, while the implications on lending activity and hence broader economic activity shouldn't be underestimated. However, even if - and that's a big if - the Federal Reserve engages in yield curve control at some point (thereby lowering long-term rates), U.S. banks as a whole should continue to be able to generate meaningful net interest income thanks to the efficiency gains achieved in the ultra-low-interest rate environment of the post-GFC period.

On a side note, BAC's net interest margin, as illustrated in red in Figure 6, should be taken with a grain of salt. The bank doesn't report the number directly, so I had to calculate it based on reported net interest income and average earning assets.

Comparing borrowing costs among the "Big 5" proved quite difficult due to the varying information provided in the earnings report supplements. Therefore, I confine the discuss in this regard to JPM, WFC, and USB. Figure 7 shows that total borrowing costs declined for the first time in 2025 after having risen sharply since 2022 amid the Federal Reserve's interest rate increases. A closer look at the figures for JPM and WFC reveals that the decline in 2025 is largely due to lower interest payments on deposits and lower short-term lending rates. As mentioned above, JPM in particular is likely to continue to benefit from its position as the largest bank in the U.S. and will certainly be able to maintain a substantial net interest margin even if interest rates continue to fall.

Unlike more conventional banks such as Truist Financial (following the sale of its insurance business), JPM, BAC, C, WFC, and USB continue to generate significant non-interest income (Figure 8), which reduces the banks' interest rate sensitivity, of course leaving aside secondary effects stemming from interest rate policy changes.

Of these five banks, I currently only own USB, which I initiated a position in during the banking crisis in early 2023. However, I have JPM on my watch list due to its consistently impressive performance despite - but of course also because of - its size. What is currently preventing me from opening a position is the valuation of JPM stock. With a price-to-book ratio of almost 2.5, JPM is trading more than 30% above its five-year average valuation. The shares also appear expensive from a price-to-earnings ratio perspective.

Table 1 shows that, from a historical perspective, all of the "Big 4" banks in the U.S. are currently somewhat expensive. The market is likely once again pricing in the tailwinds from deregulation and ongoing consolidation. At the same time, while it is debatable whether comparing current valuations with the five-year average makes sense, even from a longer-term perspective (i.e., the last ten years), USB stock is the only one that appears to be fairly valued. Against this backdrop, I tend to view the negative reactions to last week's (generally solid) earnings reports as a sign that the market had previously been a little too generous in its praise of the banks. From this perspective, and assuming that USB does not report disappointing results for the fourth quarter of 2025 today or issue cautious guidance for 2026, USB shares should be treated more favorably by the market.

Conclusion: My Expectations For 2026 And Why I'm Unlikely To Buy Bank Stocks

Judging by the fourth quarter and full-year 2025 results of the four largest U.S. banks, JPM, BAC, C, and WFC, the banking industry as a whole appears to be entering 2026 on solid footing. Banks are well capitalized, and the spread between borrowing costs and interest rates on loans, leases, and investments remains significant despite interest rate cuts.

Over the past 15 years, banks have learned how to operate profitably in a low-interest rate environment. Personally, I expect long-term interest rates to remain sticky for the foreseeable future, while short-term rates are likely to continue to decline. This is likely to have a negative impact on the underwriting of correspondingly expensive long-term loans (e.g., in the real estate sector), while banks will continue to generate significant income from maturity transformation in such an environment.

In my view, all major banks are well positioned in the current environment, thanks in part to their significant non-interest income. However, JPM is particularly well positioned with its leading returns on tangible common equity and its extremely solid asset management business. Nevertheless, I remain similarly confident in the much smaller but equally well-diversified U.S. Bancorp, which continues to benefit from less stringent regulatory requirements due to its non-G-SIB status and smaller asset base. Of course, its growth prospects are comparatively limited, as are its economies of scale. Overall, the longer-term outlook for all five of the largest banks in the U.S. is solid, with average consensus earnings growth rates in the high single digits:

Although a full-blown recession is far from certain, there are several signs that consumers are coming under increasing pressure, such as rising provisions for loan defaults in the consumer credit sector. A return to once again higher inflation rates could be driven by ongoing build-up of redundancies amid onshoring and the failure to reduce bureaucratic overhead. That said, thanks to very solid overall stock market returns in recent years, the broad middle class and upper class remain healthy spenders, in my view. Conversely, however, a significant - and prolonged - correction could have considerable secondary effects, and in such a scenario, banks would naturally be among the first to suffer – especially in terms of stock performance.

Corporate spending and the associated banking business continue to look solid from an asset quality perspective. However, due to ongoing uncertainties related to tariffs and the resulting reluctance to make significant investments (aside of AI-related investments), I expect relatively moderate growth in loan underwriting activity in 2026.

Personally, I am entering 2026 with mixed feelings about the U.S. economy and the world as a whole. I continue to invest, more or less, a fixed amount each month in sectors where I see opportunities. From a valuation perspective and given the mixed macroeconomic outlook, I do not currently expect to increase my bank holdings, despite the undoubtedly continued solid performance of most banks and their strong capitalization. However, should there be a correction that brings valuations closer to their historical average, I could imagine starting a position in JPM stock, which I still consider to be a particularly well-managed bank with surprisingly solid growth despite its size.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Greenland: We Have A Problem
data.description:
Just when it looked like markets were calming down, the tariff-related drama is rattling markets again. As of early Monday morning, the S&P 500 futures "SPX" (SP500) were down by roughly 1%, the Nasdaq 100 futures were down by about 1.5%, gold was up by about 2%, and silver was surging by about 5%.

Remarkably, silver futures hit nearly $95 in early Monday trade, creeping ever closer to that $100 mark. In any case, the risk-off feel is back, as President Trump makes claims to "acquire" Greenland and has even put higher tariffs back on the table for several European nations.

We already learned that the market wasn't crazy about tariffs early last year. So, we're also seeing yields rising, with the 10-year above 4.2%, and the 30-year jacked up back above 4.8% on Monday morning. Now, how are mortgage and other key rates supposed to come down with Treasuries trending higher again? Additionally, the Europeans aren't sitting on their hands here, and the response has been quite unified and negative over the escalation regarding Greenland. Now, the EU is contemplating answering with a $100+ billion tariff package to counter the U.S. tariff threat.

Also, the EU owns a lot of U.S. debt, stocks, and many other assets, and its U.S. Treasury holdings could be Europe's "big stick," Dutch Bank thinks. Theoretically, EU countries could dump their U.S. Treasuries and other assets, potentially causing volatility to spike, especially in the near term here.

Moreover, it's now becoming clearer that Kevin Warsh may be named as the next Fed Chair, which is considerably less dovish than the appointment of Kevin Hassett would be, in my view.

Earnings season, economic indicators, and an upcoming FOMC event are additional factors that may increase near-term uncertainty, which may lead to increased volatility.

However, despite these near-term issues potentially leading to more transitory volatility in the near term, I remain bullish in the intermediate and longer term.

The resilient economic backdrop, continued expansion in AI and other crucial segments, easier monetary policy, lower taxes, deregulation, and other constructive elements should drive high-quality stocks higher in 2026. Therefore, I am keeping my 2026 year-end S&P 500 target at 8,000.

Technically: Near-Term Volatility Could Increase

The SPX is around a critical support area, which is at 6,900-6,800. Now, with the increase in volatility, the SPX may even temporarily dip below 6,800. However, if the 6,700 support is breached, the SPX and markets in general could go through a more extensive correction process. Provided the setup, if 6,700 support is breached, we may see a pullback down to roughly 6,500-6,300 in a worst-case outcome.

Volatility Over Greenland

Markets hate uncertainty, and the tariff escalation over the situation in Greenland introduces significant near-term uncertainties. The U.S. President appears intent on acquiring the island, and unless a compromise with the EU allies is achieved soon, this situation could disrupt financial markets. While a repeat of last April's meltdown isn't likely, in my view, a 5-10% correction could potentially occur.

Earnings Season To Heat Up

We've seen several key reports, and most big banks, as well as other early bellwether companies, have reported better than anticipated top and especially bottom line results. This week, we have Netflix, Inc. (NFLX), 3M Company (MMM), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), The Charles Schwab Corporation (SCHW), General Electric Company (GE), and other critical companies reporting.

Then, we get to peak earnings season around the end of January and the beginning of next month. Positive tech and other critical earnings could serve as a positive catalyst to limit the geopolitical-related uncertainty and potentially reflect positively on high-quality stocks.

Upcoming Crucial Data

We have key, crucial data this week, especially on Wednesday and Thursday. The President's speech on Wednesday morning may be a market-moving event. Also, we have GDP, PCE, and other essential data coming in this week, which could move markets. Especially important is the core PCE, as we would prefer to see lower than expected inflation. A 2.7% would be okay, but I believe the market would really like to see 2.6%, which could increase near-term rate cut probabilities.

The Bottom Line: Valuations Could Expand

While the trailing P/E ratios may appear elevated, the forward P/E ratios are not, provided the potential solid growth and the easier monetary environment. The SPX has a forward P/E ratio of 22, which is relatively inexpensive, provided the environment and the economic backdrop. Moreover, the Nasdaq 100 forward P/E ratio is only about 25, and the Russell 2000 appears even cheaper, trading only around 24 times forward earnings estimates.

Excluding the temporary geopolitical elements and other transitory factors, multiples could expand in a solid or even moderate growth environment coupled with a more accessible monetary policy. In addition, there are favorable growth areas like AI, defense, energy, and others that could drive economic growth and corporate earnings growth. Therefore, stocks have a high probability to trend higher, and I am keeping my 8,000 SPX target for year-end 2026.

data.title: Nebius: I Was All Wrong About AI Infrastructure Demand (Upgrade)
data.description:
When I covered Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) last time, I was quite confident that it was an easy "Strong Sell" because it seemed too expensive at $11+ billion market cap. At the moment, Nebius Group is worth more than $27 billion, meaning that my call was all wrong. The stock skyrocketed after the massive AI infrastructure deal was announced in early September 2025. My bearish opinion was mostly based on the idea that NBIS is too small to compete with the U.S. and Chinese tech giants in the AI infrastructure business. However, it turned out that the demand is so high that even giants like Microsoft are not able to meet it without outsourcing. There are numerous recent developments showing us that AI infrastructure demand is poised to accelerate further as the number of AI use cases is expanding rapidly. Valuation remains the major risk due to high execution risks, but there is a massive share price momentum on the other hand. All in all, I plan to open a relatively small NBIS position and will be doubling down in case of notable sentiment-driven dips. All in all, I am upgrading NBIS to "Buy".

Recent developments

As I mentioned in the introduction, NBIS soared after the large AI infrastructure deal with Microsoft was announced. It happened in early September, meaning that it was already covered by many other Seeking Alpha analysts. So, I will share my opinion on it very briefly. A $17+ billion deal with the world's second largest cloud infrastructure player and one of the most iconic technological companies in history is not just about financials, but it is a massive validation victory for Nebius. By the way, Nebius also has a $3 billion AI infrastructure deal with Meta Platforms. It is almost six times smaller dollar-wise than the Microsoft deal, but it is still a multi-billion contract with another tech giant. I think that there is nothing bigger for a young technological company like NBIS from validation perspective other than a multi-billion contract with one of the companies representing the "Magnificent 7" group. It means that your company has been recognized as being in the same league as them from technological perspective. And Nebius already boasts two contracts with Mag 7 companies, not including deepening technological partnership with Nvidia.

Therefore, I am not surprised that NBIS enjoys such a strong share price momentum. The stock is up by staggering 415% over the last nine months, which is absolutely jaw-dropping. NBIS is down by roughly 12% over the last three months, but I think that this share price weakness was mostly sentiment-driven due to Nvidia's (NVDA) relatively weak share price performance over the same period. Moreover, there is still uncertainty about fairness of Nebius' valuation, which I will discuss in a separate section.

I think that the sell-off of the last three months was mostly sentiment-driven because industry developments were all positive for NBIS over the last few months. We have figured out at the beginning of this passage that with large deals with Microsoft and Meta, Nebius already boasts reputation as a five-star player in the AI infrastructure market. And the market is poised to continue growing rapidly given several recent developments. During the CES 2026 event, Nvidia's management made several bullish statements regarding still strong AI-related demand. Their positive outlook was recently reinforced by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company's (TSM) strong AI-driven Q4 earnings together with the fact that Taiwan is set to invest at least $250 billion in semiconductor, energy, and AI production in the U.S.

There are other indications that the world needs much more compute power for AI, which we can see from recent statements from prominent AI memory chip players like Micron (MU) and Samsung Electronics (SSNLF). Micron recently said that the global memory chip shortage is "unprecedented", and it is likely to last beyond 2026. Top managers of Samsung are also very bullish as they expect to see a 3x YoY increase in Q4 2025 operating profit thanks to the booming AI-related memory chip business. Having such bullish statements from various large players in the industry is unlikely to be just hype or something unsustainable. I think that the logic behind soaring AI compute power is really simple. The size of AI infrastructure market will depend on the number of use cases for artificial intelligence, and this number appears to be growing quite aggressively.

BCG's "AI Radar 2026" is a quite interesting survey, which I consider representative, as it is a comprehensive research across various countries, industries, and company sizes. It researched views of 2,360 respondents, mostly C-suite executives. Therefore, I think that the source is highly reliable in assessing the power of AI momentum. Apart from the key takeaways summarized in the above slide, I also believe it is crucial to mention that the report also cites that the survey cites a 2x forecasted increase in AI-related investments [on average from ~0.8% of revenue in 2025 to ~1.7% in 2026]. Such a significant expected spike in terms of AI investments likely means that business leaders see great potential to improve processes within their companies by incorporating AI capabilities. Since the survey covers companies from various industries, it is highly likely that the number of use AI cases has great potential to expand further.

With such strong industry tailwinds, I expect Nebius to deliver new large positive surprises. Every new large AI infrastructure contract will highly likely lead to substantial upgrades from Wall Street analysts to Nebius' earnings estimates.

Valuation remains a major risk despite the upgrade

While I believe that massive share price momentum together with accelerating AI adoption is a powerful combination to push NBIS share price significantly higher, I am still concerned about its valuation. A $27+ billion market cap for a company with negative EPS is a very generous valuation even though Nebius' Price/Sales ratio is expected to compress dramatically over the next few years. The fact that Nebius already has contracts with Microsoft and Meta cumulatively worth more than $20 billion makes a projected rapid P/S ratio contraction highly probable.

However, Nebius doesn't have a proven record of converting aggressive revenue growth into bottom line profitability. While revenue growth is a vital metric for a tech company, it might make no difference for investors over the long-term if NBIS fails to deliver operational leverage. Current generous valuation of Nebius implies that investors expect the company to generate superior returns over the long term.

The biggest risk at this stage of development is execution risk. First and foremost, delivering quality and value for Microsoft and Meta means that Nebius has no room for material error during the AI infrastructure buildout phase. Not meeting schedules agreed with its largest customers might lead to deteriorated business relationships and flawed reputation. Moreover, missing deadlines and budgets will also mean that the company's actual profits from these large deals might be lower-than-expected. Due to the "priced to perfection status" of NBIS investors might punish the stock with large selloffs if there are doubts about the company's ability to keep its largest customers happy.

Bottom line

To conclude, I think that NBIS has potential to soar further given massive share price momentum together with positive developments around the AI infrastructure industry. On the other hand, valuation fairness is a big question, which might be a massive red flag if the entire U.S. equity market decides it is time to play defensive. Therefore, assigning a "Buy" rating together with initiating a relatively small position is a reasonable strategy.

data.title: Microsoft: The $134 Billion Misunderstanding
data.description:
Headlines emerged on Saturday that Elon Musk is seeking damages of $79B-$134B from OpenAI (OPENAI) and Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), alleging that the maker of ChatGPT defrauded the Tesla CEO (NASDAQ:TSLA). The case is fast-tracking to an April jury trial in Oakland, with Musk claiming his 2015 donation of $38M entitles him to partial ownership of the for-profit OpenAI. In 2015, OpenAI had non-profit status; however, in 2019 a for-profit subsidiary was launched with the OpenAI Foundation remaining non-profit.

Legal concerns notwithstanding, investors should look past the headline noise. Filings reveal that Microsoft's exposure is capped at a maximum of ~$25B. While a considerable sum, this figure represents roughly two months of operating cash flow or less than 25% of the company's $102B war chest comprised of cash, equivalents, and short-term investments.

While Microsoft owns 27% of OpenAI, the primary target of the lawsuit is OpenAI. Thus, the real risk to Microsoft is not a fine but a potential delay to OpenAI's IPO. At present, the market's reaction has created a disconnect between sentiment and fundamentals.

Despite the headline risk, now it is time to buy the dip on this 'Magnificent 7' leader. While the stock has declined approximately 20% from its highs, catalysts have emerged, including:

  • Valuation Support: Trading near $455/share, Microsoft is valued at 28.3x 2026 earnings with an anticipated 19.2% in earnings growth. This valuation represents the low end of its recent historical range, presenting investors with a good margin of safety.
  • Growth: Microsoft remains the premier hyperscaler, with 16% revenue growth anticipated in 2026. With the AI boom still in its early innings, the company has ample runway in Azure, Cloud, and Co-Pilot to fuel growth in the coming years.
  • Sentiment Washout: Following the Michael Burry 'big short' thesis on AI and the recent lawsuit, Microsoft is approaching a bottom in sentiment. The lawsuit concern is capped, and I believe Burry is wrong or early on his AI bubble call, presenting investors with a contrarian entry.
  • Earnings Catalyst: Expectations for Q2 2026 earnings (Jan. 28th) present a low bar for revenue ($80.3B) and EPS ($3.86/share). If Microsoft follows last year's seasonality and sustains its 33% Azure growth, a beat is highly probable.
  • OpenAI IPO: Microsoft's 27% stake in OpenAI remains a potential upside catalyst. With a $1T IPO rumored for late 2026 or early 2027, the repricing of this asset is currently not reflected in the stock price.

In total, the present valuation and catalysts for the coming year position Microsoft as a Strong Buy. Shares offer 12-month upside to $600/share, a potential 32% return.

Microsoft: Q4 2025 Earnings Preview

For the coming quarterly earnings report, the bar is set too low. In the most recent quarter, Microsoft saw revenue expand to $77.7 billion, with diluted GAAP EPS reaching $3.72/share (up 17% and 13% YoY). The table below (from the latest 10-Q) shows the most recent growth in Microsoft's business segments.

The current consensus estimates call for $80.3 billion in revenue and $3.86/share in EPS. Microsoft's historical seasonality suggests fiscal Q2 (ending in Jan.) is generally the strongest due to year-end budget flushing and strength in the holiday period. In Q2 2025, revenue increased 12% YoY to $69.6 billion, while EPS increased 10% to $3.23/share (6.1% QoQ sales growth from Q1 2025). Thus, in the coming quarter, Wall Street projects a sales increase of $2.6 billion from the $77.7 billion reported in September (3.3% QoQ growth). If Microsoft matches its typical seasonal velocity, revenue would land near $82.4 billion, clearing the consensus bar by over $2 billion.

The primary engine for this beat is Azure. In Q3 2025, Azure growth accelerated to 40%, debunking the "deceleration" narrative that plagued the stock in recent months. Microsoft's net margin in Q2 2025 was 34.6%, recording $24.1 billion in net income from revenues of $69.6 billion.

The math for the bottom line is tighter than the top line. To hit consensus $3.86/share EPS targets, Microsoft needs a slight margin expansion to 34.8% with share count constant since last year ($82.4 billion in revenue at 34.8% margin realizes $28.5 billion in net income or $3.86/share with 7.4324 billion shares). While possible, expect investors to monitor Capex changes closely for clues. Increasing capital investment spending is a risk to the stock in the coming quarter, as investors have reacted negatively to any increase.

Microsoft Sentiment: The Musk and Burry Discount

The headline risk of Elon Musk’s seeking up to $134 billion in damages harms sentiment, but the financial reality is far less concerning. Court filings clarify that Microsoft’s specific exposure is capped between $13.3 billion and $25.1 billion.

To put this "worst-case" scenario in perspective, we turn to Microsoft’s balance sheet. As of Sept. 2025, the company held $102 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments. Thus, the company could pay the maximum estimated damages four times over without touching its credit lines. From another perspective, Microsoft realized $34.2 billion in operating cash flow in just the last quarter.

While I am not a legal expert, Musk's claim of up to $134 billion in damages (13.4% of a potential $1T IPO) seems disproportionate to his $38 million investment. While 2015 was very early (representing up to 60% of OpenAI's seed funding), Microsoft has committed up to "$13 billion, with $11.6 billion funded as of Sept. 30, 2025," according to the latest 10-Q. The disparity between the headline number and the actual investment suggests the final settlement will likely be a fraction of what Musk is seeking. Finally, the jury will be selected in Oakland, California, which may bode poorly for Mr. Musk.
